metadata.title: Systematic errors in the perception of rhythm
metadata.note: Original psychophysical study examining systematic errors in rhythm perception using isochronic beat patterns. 7 participants, varying number of beats (3, 4, 6, 9) and inter-beat intervals (0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2 s). Bayesian computational model proposed to explain systematic errors.

claims.claim_text: Observers perceived the time of the last beat of a rhythmic pattern as happening earlier than actual when the inter-beat interval was short (0.15-0.3 s), with subjects too often responding 'Early' even when the last beat came late

dependent_variable.name: Systematic timing error (early vs late bias)

axis_proposal.definition: Systematic error in perception of beat timing, measured as bias towards 'Early' or 'Late' responses when last beat has no deviation (rho=0)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Difference between number of 'Late' and 'Early' responses when last beat has no deviation, normalized by total responses. Positive values indicate 'Late' bias, negative values indicate 'Early' bias.

statistics.note: For inter-beat intervals of 0.15 and 0.3 s, asymmetry statistically significant with large effect size when final deviation is 5% (n=188, p<2×10^-8, Cohen's h=1.1)

methodology.task: Listening to isochronic beat patterns with varying number of beats (N) and inter-beat intervals (Delta). Last beat was either earlier or later than expected (relative deviation rho). Task: indicate whether final beat was too early or too late.
methodology.control_conditions: Within-subjects design with varying N (3, 4, 6, 9), Delta (0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2 s), and rho (-0.15 to +0.15 in steps)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Neurologically healthy, normal hearing, ages 16-64, 3 females, 4 males. Two participants in lab, five on own computers. Five naive to purpose of experiment.

claims.claim_text: The perceived time of the last beat was later than the actual when the inter-beat interval was long (0.9-1.2 s), with the asymmetry reversing direction for longer intervals

dependent_variable.name: Systematic timing error (early vs late bias)

axis_proposal.definition: Systematic error in perception of beat timing, measured as bias towards 'Early' or 'Late' responses when last beat has no deviation (rho=0)
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Difference between number of 'Late' and 'Early' responses when last beat has no deviation, normalized by total responses. Positive values indicate 'Late' bias, negative values indicate 'Early' bias.

statistics.note: For inter-beat interval of 1.2 s, reversal always statistically significant with large effect size (n=102, p<2×10^-5, Cohen's h=-1.6)

methodology.task: Listening to isochronic beat patterns with varying number of beats (N) and inter-beat intervals (Delta). Last beat was either earlier or later than expected (relative deviation rho). Task: indicate whether final beat was too early or too late.
methodology.control_conditions: Within-subjects design with varying N (3, 4, 6, 9), Delta (0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2 s), and rho (-0.15 to +0.15 in steps)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Neurologically healthy, normal hearing, ages 16-64, 3 females, 4 males. Two participants in lab, five on own computers. Five naive to purpose of experiment.

claims.claim_text: The magnitude of systematic errors fell as the number of beats increased, with performance improving (fewer asymmetric early-vs-late biases) with larger number of beats

axis_proposal.definition: Probability of correctly determining whether last beat was too early or too late, measured as percentage of correct responses
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Two-alternative forced choice task accuracy in detecting timing deviations of final beat in isochronic rhythm patterns

statistics.note: Difference between combined 3-and-4-beats data and combined 6-and-9-beats results statistically significant with small effect size (n=680, p<0.002, Cohen's h=0.3). Remaining asymmetry with 9 beats still significant with medium effect size (n=196, p<0.004 for 0.15 s, Cohen's h=0.6)

methodology.task: Listening to isochronic beat patterns with varying number of beats (N) and inter-beat intervals (Delta). Last beat was either earlier or later than expected (relative deviation rho). Task: indicate whether final beat was too early or too late.
methodology.control_conditions: Within-subjects design with varying N (3, 4, 6, 9), Delta (0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2 s), and rho (-0.15 to +0.15 in steps)
methodology.participant_characteristics: Neurologically healthy, normal hearing, ages 16-64, 3 females, 4 males. Two participants in lab, five on own computers. Five naive to purpose of experiment.

claims.claim_text: A Bayesian model incorporating temporally asymmetric impulse responses, prior distributions preferring certain time intervals, and Better-Early-than-Late loss functions fits the systematic errors well, suggesting these errors may be optimal from a probabilistic perspective

axis_proposal.definition: Goodness of fit of Bayesian model to experimental data, measured by χ² statistic
axis_proposal.psychoacoustic_basis: Statistical measure of how well computational model predictions match observed behavioral data in rhythm timing perception

statistics.note: Model with Absolute-value Loss (L1) had χ²=56.2, Margin-based Loss (LM) had χ²=56.2, Quadratic Loss (L2) had χ²=58.5. Maximum-a-posteriori Loss (Lδ) had χ²=159.0 (almost three times worse). Model was statistically indistinguishable from data. Optimal parameters: n=1, τI=75 ms, τE=15 ms, τD=1.4 s, σ=1.1

brain_regions.context: Functional significance in predictive coding of temporal information. Key role in absolute timing measurements for beat perception with two pulses.
Involved in temporal prediction and rhythm processing
Involved in temporal prediction and rhythm processing
Involved in temporal prediction and rhythm processing
Involved in temporal prediction and rhythm processing
Temporally asymmetric impulse responses in auditory system, particularly in auditory nerve and cortex

methodology.task: Bayesian model to predict systematic errors in rhythm timing perception. Model incorporates: (1) temporally asymmetric impulse response (Gamma distribution), (2) prior distribution (lognormal) preferring certain time intervals, (3) Better-Early-than-Late loss function (BELL)
methodology.control_conditions: Model compared to experimental data. Different loss functions tested: Margin-based (LM), Quadratic (L2), Absolute-value (L1), Maximum-a-posteriori (Lδ)
methodology.analysis_details: Model optimized using coordinate-descent algorithm by minimizing χ². Parameters: τI (impulse response time constant), n (asymmetry parameter), τ∆ (prior time constant), σ (prior symmetry), τE (BELL parameter), τS (margin parameter)

c3_associations.process: Systematic errors in rhythm perception depend on inter-beat interval, with short intervals causing early bias and long intervals causing late bias, suggesting optimal Bayesian estimation of beat timing

brain_regions.role: Predictive coding of temporal information, absolute timing measurements

c3_associations.evidence: Short inter-beat intervals (0.15-0.3 s, ~200-400 bpm) cause systematic early bias. Long inter-beat intervals (0.9-1.2 s, ~50-67 bpm) cause systematic late bias. Errors decrease with more beats. Bayesian model suggests optimal estimation despite systematic errors, incorporating temporally asymmetric impulse responses, prior distributions, and Better-Early-than-Late loss functions.

c3_associations.process: Bayesian predictive coding model explains systematic timing errors through optimal probabilistic estimation, incorporating prior beliefs about time intervals and loss functions that optimize survival goals
c3_associations.evidence: Bayesian model fits data well (χ²=56.2 for L1 loss). Model incorporates: (1) temporally asymmetric impulse responses (Gamma distribution, n=1, τI=75 ms), (2) prior distribution (lognormal, τ∆=1.4 s, σ=1.1) preferring intervals around 2.6 s, (3) Better-Early-than-Late loss function (τE=15 ms). Model suggests systematic errors are optimal from probabilistic perspective, optimizing survival goals rather than accuracy.

primary_axes.note: Inter-beat intervals converted to bpm: 1.2 s = 50 bpm, 0.9 s = 67 bpm, 0.6 s = 100 bpm, 0.3 s = 200 bpm, 0.15 s = 400 bpm. Short intervals (0.15-0.3 s) cause early bias, long intervals (0.9-1.2 s) cause late bias. Least systematic errors around 0.6 s (100 bpm), matching popular music tempos (90-99 bpm).

r3_coordinates.note: Inter-beat interval (Delta) is the primary independent variable, converted to tempo (bpm) for R³ mapping. Number of beats (N) also affects systematic errors but is not directly mappable to R³. Bayesian model suggests optimal estimation despite systematic errors, with errors depending on prior beliefs (preferring intervals around 2.6 s), temporally asymmetric impulse responses, and Better-Early-than-Late loss functions.

mentions.context: Functional significance in predictive coding of temporal information. Key role in absolute timing measurements for beat perception with two pulses.
Predictive coding of temporal information, absolute timing measurements
Involved in temporal prediction and rhythm processing

brain_regions_summary.note: This is a psychophysical study with no direct neuroimaging. All brain region mentions are from literature review and theoretical discussion. Brain regions mentioned are those known to be involved in temporal prediction and rhythm processing based on previous research. The study focuses on behavioral systematic errors and computational modeling rather than direct neural measurements.

warnings: 3 DV r3_axis unmapped (axis_proposal provided for all)
Psychophysical study with no direct neuroimaging (all brain regions from literature inference)
Small sample size (n=7) but power analysis conducted

data_quality.note: Original psychophysical study with 7 participants. All statistics reported (binomial tests, p-values, Cohen's h effect sizes). Brain regions identified through literature review (no direct neuroimaging). R³ coordinates mapped for inter-beat interval converted to tempo (L6.tempo_bpm). Bayesian computational model provides theoretical framework for systematic errors.
